runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate: a 1% increase in the capital-gains marginal tax rate changes realizations by this proportion.
citations: Saez (2010) on elasticities of capital gains realization to tax rates

runs.reasoningSummary: In the medium run, realizations typically respond positively to increases in the marginal capital-gains tax rate (or equivalently, decline when the rate falls) due to tax-motivated timing, but the response is usually only partial because of portfolio constraints, underlying gains, and transaction frictions. I center the elasticity in the moderate range (~0.5–0.6) with a plausible spread from weak to relatively strong responses.

I center the elasticity in the moderate range (~0.5–0.6) with a plausible spread from weak to relatively strong responses."
}

runs.reasoningSummary: I expect a moderate-to-high realization response to marginal tax rate changes in the medium run: taxpayers can time realizations and respond to after-tax incentives, but some realizations are constrained by holding motives and portfolio needs, so the elasticity is usually positive and not unbounded. I place the median near ~0.85 with a wide but plausible spread.
runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation":"Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate, measured in the U.S. for individuals with long-term capital gains (a 1% increase in the marginal tax rate changes realizations by this percent).","point_estimate":0.85,"quantiles":{"p05":0.25,"p25":0.55,"p50":0.85,"p75":1.10,"p95":1.60},"citations":["Kiefer and Weiss (capital gains realization response to taxation)","Saez (capital gains bunching/realization elasticities; bunching methodology)","Feldstein, Slemrod, and Yitzhaki (tax effects on capital gains realizations; elasticity concepts)"],"reasoning_summary":"I expect a moderate-to-high realization response to marginal tax rate changes in the medium run: taxpayers can time realizations and respond to after-tax incentives, but some realizations are constrained by holding motives and portfolio needs, so the elasticity is usually positive and not unbounded. I place the median near ~0.85 with a wide but plausible spread."}

citations: Papers estimating realization responses to capital gains tax changes (e.g., classic findings from Auerbach and colleagues / related studies on tax-induced realizations)
Taxpayer response and bunching/realization timing evidence around capital gains rate changes in the U.S.
General behavioral/portfolio adjustment framing from structural and reduced-form tax elasticity literature
runs.reasoningSummary: I place the median medium-run elasticity slightly below 1, with moderate uncertainty: realization behavior responds positively to higher marginal tax rates (i.e., higher rates reduce realizations), but the magnitude varies by cohort, assets, and the degree to which investors can defer sales and rebalance over the medium run.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate in a U.S. tax model.
citations: Recency/earmark of Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) on elasticity evidence for realization responses
Recall of classic estimates around the realized capital gains tax elasticity range (often ~0.5–1.5) from behavioral/tax-motivated timing responses
General economic mechanism: bunching/lock-in implies positive but heterogeneous elasticity; medium-run often stronger than very short-run
runs.reasoningSummary: I endorse a positive, moderately sized medium-run response: some households have large opportunities to time realizations (bunching/lock-in), but behavior is heterogeneous and constrained by asset sales needs and portfolio composition, leading to an elasticity centered near ~0.9 with plausible spread roughly 0.2–1.8.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate for U.S. individuals.
citations: Behavioral responses to capital-gains taxation and realization bunching around tax changes (e.g., evidence from policy/tax-rate reforms)
Theoretical work on realizations timing under lock-in and tax-rate changes (standard realization elasticity / lock-in model intuitions)
Empirical estimates in public finance literature that typically find positive but not extremely large medium-run elasticities
runs.reasoningSummary: Tax-rate increases generally reduce realizations in a timing/lock-in framework, implying a positive elasticity. For the medium run, I expect moderate responsiveness (less than unity but clearly above zero), reflecting both realized-gains timing and partial offsetting behavior (portfolio rebalancing, deferral) rather than full one-for-one responses.
runs.rawResponse:
{
  "interpretation": "Medium-run elasticity of long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate for U.S. individuals.",
  "point_estimate": 0.45,
  "quantiles": {
    "p05": 0.15,
    "p25": 0.30,
    "p50": 0.45,
    "p75": 0.65,
    "p95": 1.00
  },
  "citations": [
    "Behavioral responses to capital-gains taxation and realization bunching around tax changes (e.g., evidence from policy/tax-rate reforms)",
    "Theoretical work on realizations timing under lock-in and tax-rate changes (standard realization elasticity / lock-in model intuitions)",
    "Empirical estimates in public finance literature that typically find positive but not extremely large medium-run elasticities"
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Tax-rate increases generally reduce realizations in a timing/lock-in framework, implying a positive elasticity. For the medium run, I expect moderate responsiveness (less than unity but clearly above zero), reflecting both realized-gains timing and partial offsetting behavior (portfolio rebalancing, deferral) rather than full one-for-one responses."
}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of U.S. individuals’ long-term capital gains realizations with respect to the capital-gains marginal tax rate.
citations: Emanuel Saez, Joel Slemrod, and Seth Giertz (summary evidence on tax responsiveness/realizations)
Classic capital gains bunching/realization response literature (e.g., behavioral & realization elasticity findings around rate changes)
U.S. capital gains tax policy change evidence (responses often negative and modest-to-moderate in magnitude)
runs.reasoningSummary: I assume the elasticity is negative (higher marginal tax rates reduce realizations) and—on a medium-run horizon—larger than very short-run timing effects but smaller than extreme anticipation effects, yielding a median around -0.55 with plausible spread from about -0.95 to -0.10.
